evaluation_inputs.system_description: A large-scale, complex software system currently in Spiral 1 development, intended for significant future enhancement including Net-Ready Key Performance Parameters (NR-KPP). The system utilizes a hybrid architecture combining new N-tier structures with substantial legacy code, documented primarily through hierarchical UML and Requisite Pro artifacts.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Standard contract documents (CONOPS, SRS, SSDD) were available in paper/electronic document format. However, native electronic design data (Rational Rose, Requisite Pro) was not accessible, making the hierarchical architecture difficult to browse.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What are the driving architectural constraints, and where are they documented?
How do components communicate and synchronize?
What are the styles of architectural approaches?

hardware_view_description: System must support portability across Linux and Windows NT platforms
Low-level I/O interface layer defined in N-tier architecture to handle platform differences
runtime_constraints: System has a statically set limit for the number of simultaneous connections (links)
Legacy code imposes single-threaded control models on many components

questions: What is the process/thread model of the architecture?
What are the system states and modes?
What variability points are included in the architecture?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Introduction of a Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) for data sharing and enterprise services.
System needs to support a number of links beyond the currently anticipated statically set limit.
Integration of Web Service (WS) based modules across system components.
Replacement of a current component with a known external interface.

business_goals: Achieve Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) compliance
Establish an Open Software Architecture design with layered interfaces
Create an integration model for legacy code, new code, and COTS products
technical_constraints: Limited access to electronic CASE tool data
Short timeline and limited resources for assessment (necessitating a hybrid ATAM)

architectural_approaches.description: Multi-layered architecture with low-level I/O interface layer for platform abstraction, separating concerns across presentation, business logic, and data access tiers

addressed_attributes.rationale: Separation of concerns across tiers enables independent development and maintenance of each layer
Tiered structure allows individual layers to be scaled independently based on load requirements
New functionality can be added to specific tiers without affecting other layers

architectural_approaches.description: Low-level I/O interface layer that abstracts platform-specific differences between Linux and Windows NT

addressed_attributes.rationale: Enables the system to operate across multiple platforms (Linux and Windows NT) without code duplication
Allows platform changes or additions with minimal impact to upper layers

architectural_approaches.description: Integration pattern combining legacy code, new code, and COTS products with single-threaded control models for legacy components

addressed_attributes.rationale: Allows incremental modernization while preserving existing functionality from legacy systems
Leverages proven legacy code while adding new capabilities, reducing risk of complete system rewrites

architectural_approaches.approach_name: Open Software Architecture with Layered Interfaces
architectural_approaches.description: Hierarchical UML-based design structure with well-defined interfaces between layers, supporting modularity and component independence

addressed_attributes.rationale: Clear interface definitions enable components to be developed, tested, and replaced independently
Standardized interfaces facilitate integration with external systems and COTS products
Well-defined interfaces allow components to be modified or replaced without cascading changes

children.refinement: Data sharing with enterprise services (NR-KPP compliance)
children.scenario_description: Introduction of a Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) for data sharing and enterprise services

children.refinement: Web Service integration across system components
children.scenario_description: Integration of Web Service (WS) based modules across system components
System operates seamlessly across Linux and Windows NT platforms without platform-specific code duplication

children.refinement: Dynamic link scaling beyond static limits
children.scenario_description: System needs to support a number of links beyond the currently anticipated statically set limit
System must accommodate 200% growth in throughput, data volume, and concurrent operations
Individual N-Tier architecture layers scale independently based on load requirements without affecting other tiers
Replacement of a current component with a known external interface

children.refinement: Addition of new modules without impacting existing layers
children.scenario_description: New functionality is added to a specific tier without requiring changes to other layers in the N-Tier architecture

children.refinement: Legacy code modification while maintaining stability
children.scenario_description: Modifications to legacy components are isolated and do not propagate changes across the hybrid integration model
New capabilities are added incrementally while preserving existing legacy code functionality
Commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) products are integrated into the system through standardized layered interfaces
System architecture supports future enhancements and feature additions without requiring major redesign

children.refinement: Platform changes with minimal upper-layer impact
children.scenario_description: Platform Abstraction Layer allows platform changes or additions with minimal impact to upper layers
Components are modified or replaced without cascading changes due to well-defined layered interfaces
System maintains stability and availability despite legacy code constraints and single-threaded control models
Hybrid integration model leverages proven legacy code to reduce risk while adding new capabilities
System maintains acceptable response times (< 2 seconds) for critical operations despite single-threaded legacy component constraints under normal concurrent user load
Overhead introduced by Platform Abstraction Layer for I/O-intensive operations remains below 10% across Linux and Windows NT platforms
System achieves required throughput levels despite overhead from hierarchical layered interfaces in Open Software Architecture
Integration points between COTS products and legacy code are secured against unauthorized access and data leakage
Authentication and authorization mechanisms work consistently across Linux and Windows NT platforms
Layered interface boundaries enforce security controls and access validation between architectural tiers

scenario_reference.text: Introduction of a Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) for data sharing and enterprise services

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with enterprise service integration requirements
analysis_records.stimulus: New NR-KPP compliance mandate requiring data sharing capabilities
analysis_records.response_measure: System successfully shares data with enterprise services meeting NR-KPP standards
architectural_decisions: Open Software Architecture with Layered Interfaces enables standardized integration points
N-Tier Architecture separates data access tier for independent enterprise service integration
Web Service integration capability across system components
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered interface approach provides well-defined integration points for enterprise services. The N-Tier separation allows the data access tier to be enhanced with NR-KPP compliance without affecting presentation or business logic layers. However, legacy code integration may require adapters or wrappers to meet modern enterprise service standards.

risks.description: Legacy code components may not natively support modern enterprise service protocols required by NR-KPP
risks.rationale: The Hybrid Legacy Integration Model includes single-threaded legacy components that may lack built-in support for contemporary data sharing standards, requiring significant adapter development

risks.description: Performance degradation due to multiple abstraction layers when interfacing with enterprise services
risks.rationale: Data must traverse through Platform Abstraction Layer, N-Tier layers, and legacy integration boundaries, potentially introducing latency that violates NR-KPP performance requirements

non_risks.description: Layered interface architecture supports standardized enterprise service integration
non_risks.rationale: The Open Software Architecture with well-defined interfaces provides natural integration points for enterprise services without requiring architectural changes

sensitivity_points.parameter: Number of abstraction layers between legacy code and enterprise services
Data transformation overhead at tier boundaries

conflicting_attributes: Modularity (positive: clear separation of concerns)
Performance (negative: increased latency through multiple layers)

scenario_reference.text: System must accommodate 200% growth in throughput, data volume, and concurrent operations

analysis_records.environment: High load conditions with 200% increase in throughput, data volume, and concurrent users
analysis_records.stimulus: Sustained 200% growth in system utilization metrics
analysis_records.response_measure: System maintains acceptable performance and availability under 200% increased load
architectural_decisions: N-Tier Architecture allows independent scaling of presentation, business logic, and data tiers
Platform Abstraction Layer enables horizontal scaling across multiple platforms
Layered interfaces support load distribution across components
analysis_records.reasoning: The N-Tier architecture theoretically supports independent tier scaling, allowing bottleneck layers to be scaled without affecting others. However, the presence of single-threaded legacy components in the Hybrid Legacy Integration Model creates fundamental scalability constraints that cannot be resolved through horizontal scaling alone. The 200% growth target may exceed the capacity of legacy components that cannot be parallelized.

risks.description: Single-threaded legacy components create scalability bottlenecks that cannot be resolved through horizontal scaling
risks.rationale: Legacy code with single-threaded control models cannot leverage multi-core processors or distributed architectures, creating hard limits on concurrent operation handling

risks.description: Platform Abstraction Layer may become a performance bottleneck under 200% increased I/O load
risks.rationale: The abstraction layer adds overhead to all I/O operations; under 200% load, this overhead compounds and may exceed acceptable performance thresholds

risks.description: Inter-tier communication overhead increases non-linearly with load
risks.rationale: As load increases 200%, the number of messages between N-Tier layers may increase more than proportionally, causing network and serialization bottlenecks

non_risks.description: Stateless tiers in N-Tier architecture can be horizontally scaled
non_risks.rationale: Presentation and business logic tiers that are stateless can be replicated across multiple instances to handle increased load

sensitivity_points.parameter: Percentage of processing handled by single-threaded legacy components
Inter-tier message volume and size
Platform Abstraction Layer I/O overhead percentage

conflicting_attributes: Scalability (positive: finer-grained tiers can be scaled more precisely)
Performance (negative: more tiers increase inter-tier communication overhead)

scenario_reference.text: System maintains acceptable response times (< 2 seconds) for critical operations despite single-threaded legacy component constraints under normal concurrent user load

analysis_records.environment: Normal concurrent user load with critical operations requiring sub-2-second response
analysis_records.stimulus: User initiates critical operation that involves single-threaded legacy components
analysis_records.response_measure: Response time < 2 seconds for 95th percentile of critical operations
architectural_decisions: Hybrid Legacy Integration Model isolates legacy components to minimize impact
N-Tier Architecture allows caching and optimization at each tier
Platform Abstraction Layer provides consistent I/O performance across platforms
analysis_records.reasoning: The 2-second response time target is challenging given single-threaded legacy constraints. The architecture must rely on request queuing, caching strategies, and minimizing the number of legacy component invocations per critical operation. The N-Tier structure allows intermediate layers to cache results and batch requests to legacy components. However, if critical operations require synchronous legacy component processing, the single-threaded nature creates serialization points that may violate the 2-second target under concurrent load.

risks.description: Single-threaded legacy components create serialization points that increase response time under concurrent load
risks.rationale: When multiple concurrent users invoke critical operations requiring legacy components, requests must queue, causing response times to degrade linearly with concurrency level

risks.description: Cumulative latency across N-Tier layers and Platform Abstraction Layer may exceed 2-second budget
risks.rationale: Each layer adds processing and communication overhead; the sum of these latencies across all layers may consume the entire 2-second budget before legacy component processing

risks.description: Lack of asynchronous processing patterns for legacy components limits responsiveness
risks.rationale: Single-threaded control models typically require synchronous invocation, preventing the use of asynchronous patterns that could improve perceived response time

non_risks.description: Caching at tier boundaries can reduce legacy component invocations
non_risks.rationale: The N-Tier architecture allows each tier to implement caching strategies, reducing the frequency of expensive legacy component calls

non_risks.description: Platform Abstraction Layer overhead is bounded and measurable
non_risks.rationale: The abstraction layer introduces predictable overhead that can be measured and optimized to stay within performance budgets

sensitivity_points.parameter: Number of legacy component invocations per critical operation
Concurrent user count accessing single-threaded legacy components
Cache hit ratio at tier boundaries

conflicting_attributes: Performance (positive: larger caches reduce legacy component calls)
Reliability (negative: stale cached data may violate consistency requirements)

scenario_reference.text: Modifications to legacy components are isolated and do not propagate changes across the hybrid integration model

analysis_records.environment: Development and maintenance phase with legacy component modifications
analysis_records.stimulus: Developer modifies internal implementation of a legacy component
analysis_records.response_measure: Changes remain isolated to the legacy component; no changes required in other system components
architectural_decisions: Hybrid Legacy Integration Model encapsulates legacy components behind stable interfaces
Layered interface architecture defines clear contracts between legacy and new code
Platform Abstraction Layer isolates platform-specific legacy code
analysis_records.reasoning: The architecture's success in isolating legacy modifications depends on the stability and completeness of interface definitions. Well-defined interfaces in the Open Software Architecture should prevent change propagation. However, if legacy components expose implementation details or if interfaces are tightly coupled to legacy internals, modifications may ripple through the system. The single-threaded control model constraint may also limit modification options.

risks.description: Legacy component interfaces may be tightly coupled to implementation details, causing change propagation
risks.rationale: If legacy interfaces expose internal data structures or control flow assumptions, modifications to legacy internals may require interface changes that propagate to dependent components

risks.description: Single-threaded control model constraints may prevent certain modifications without architectural changes
risks.rationale: Attempts to improve legacy component performance through parallelization would violate the single-threaded constraint, requiring changes to the integration model

non_risks.description: Layered interface boundaries provide isolation for internal legacy modifications
non_risks.rationale: The Open Software Architecture's well-defined interfaces allow legacy component internals to be modified without affecting other layers, as long as interface contracts are maintained

non_risks.description: Platform Abstraction Layer isolates platform-specific legacy code changes
non_risks.rationale: Platform-specific modifications to legacy components are contained within the abstraction layer, preventing propagation to upper layers

sensitivity_points.parameter: Interface coupling degree between legacy and new components
Percentage of legacy component functionality exposed through interfaces

tradeoff_points.parameter: Interface abstraction level for legacy components
conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (positive: higher abstraction isolates changes better)
Performance (negative: higher abstraction adds overhead and may limit optimization opportunities)

scenario_reference.text: Integration points between COTS products and legacy code are secured against unauthorized access and data leakage

analysis_records.environment: Production environment with COTS products integrated with legacy components
analysis_records.stimulus: Attempted unauthorized access or data exfiltration at COTS-legacy integration points
analysis_records.response_measure: All unauthorized access attempts are blocked; no data leakage occurs at integration boundaries
architectural_decisions: Layered interface boundaries enforce security controls between COTS and legacy code
Hybrid Legacy Integration Model defines controlled integration points
N-Tier Architecture separates security concerns across tiers
analysis_records.reasoning: Security at COTS-legacy integration points is challenging because legacy code may lack modern security features, and COTS products have their own security models that may not align with legacy assumptions. The layered architecture provides natural enforcement points for security controls, but the diversity of security models (legacy, COTS, new code) creates complexity. The single-threaded nature of legacy components may also limit the implementation of certain security patterns like concurrent authentication checks.

risks.description: Legacy components may lack modern security features required for secure COTS integration
risks.rationale: Legacy code may not support encryption, secure authentication, or authorization mechanisms expected by modern COTS products, creating security gaps at integration points

risks.description: Inconsistent security models across legacy, COTS, and new code create vulnerability surface
risks.rationale: Each component type may have different security assumptions and mechanisms, creating opportunities for security bypasses at integration boundaries

risks.description: Platform Abstraction Layer may introduce security vulnerabilities if not properly hardened
risks.rationale: The abstraction layer operates at a low level with I/O access; vulnerabilities here could bypass higher-level security controls

non_risks.description: Layered interface boundaries provide natural security enforcement points
non_risks.rationale: The Open Software Architecture's interface layers allow security controls to be implemented at each boundary, validating all cross-layer interactions

non_risks.description: N-Tier separation allows security controls to be specialized per tier
non_risks.rationale: Different tiers can implement appropriate security mechanisms for their specific threats without affecting other tiers

sensitivity_points.parameter: Number of COTS-legacy integration points
Granularity of access control at interface boundaries

tradeoff_points.parameter: Security validation depth at integration points
conflicting_attributes: Security (positive: deeper validation catches more threats)
Performance (negative: extensive validation adds latency to all integration calls)

scenario_reference.text: System needs to support a number of links beyond the currently anticipated statically set limit

analysis_records.environment: Production environment approaching static link limit
analysis_records.stimulus: Operational requirement to exceed current static link limit
analysis_records.response_measure: System dynamically supports additional links without architectural changes or service interruption
architectural_decisions: N-Tier Architecture allows data tier to be enhanced with dynamic link management
Layered interfaces support dynamic configuration without affecting upper layers
Platform Abstraction Layer enables platform-specific link scaling optimizations
analysis_records.reasoning: The static link limit suggests hard-coded constraints that may be embedded in legacy components or architectural assumptions. The layered architecture should allow the link management functionality to be enhanced in the data tier without affecting other layers. However, if the static limit is deeply embedded in legacy code or reflects fundamental architectural assumptions, exceeding it may require significant refactoring.

risks.description: Static link limits may be hard-coded in legacy components that cannot be easily modified
risks.rationale: Legacy code may have array sizes, buffer allocations, or loop bounds based on the static limit, requiring extensive code changes to support dynamic scaling

risks.description: Memory and resource allocation patterns may assume static limits
risks.rationale: The architecture may pre-allocate resources based on static limits; dynamic scaling could cause memory exhaustion or resource contention

non_risks.description: Layered architecture allows link management to be enhanced in isolated components
non_risks.rationale: If link management is properly encapsulated in specific layers, it can be enhanced to support dynamic limits without affecting other system components

sensitivity_points.parameter: Percentage of code with hard-coded link limit assumptions
Memory allocation strategy for link-related data structures

conflicting_attributes: Scalability (positive: dynamic allocation supports unlimited links)
Performance (negative: dynamic allocation adds overhead and fragmentation)

scenario_reference.text: New capabilities are added incrementally while preserving existing legacy code functionality

analysis_records.environment: Development phase with incremental feature additions
analysis_records.stimulus: Requirement to add new capability while maintaining all existing legacy functionality
analysis_records.response_measure: New capability is successfully added; all existing legacy functionality remains operational and unmodified
architectural_decisions: Hybrid Legacy Integration Model supports coexistence of legacy and new code
N-Tier Architecture allows new capabilities to be added in separate tiers
Layered interfaces enable new functionality to be added without modifying legacy layers
analysis_records.reasoning: The Hybrid Legacy Integration Model is specifically designed for this scenario, allowing new code to be added alongside legacy components. The layered architecture provides natural extension points where new functionality can be inserted. Success depends on whether new capabilities can be implemented without requiring changes to legacy component interfaces or internal behavior.

risks.description: New capabilities requiring legacy component modifications may break existing functionality
risks.rationale: If new features require changes to legacy code, the risk of introducing defects into proven legacy functionality increases significantly

risks.description: Integration complexity increases with each incremental addition
risks.rationale: As more new capabilities are added around legacy code, the integration model becomes more complex, increasing maintenance burden and potential for integration defects

non_risks.description: Hybrid integration model is designed for incremental modernization
non_risks.rationale: The architecture explicitly supports adding new code while preserving legacy functionality, providing proven patterns for coexistence

non_risks.description: Layered interfaces allow new capabilities to be added in new layers
non_risks.rationale: New functionality can be implemented in new layers that interact with legacy layers through stable interfaces, avoiding legacy code modifications

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of coupling between new capabilities and legacy code
Percentage of new functionality requiring legacy interface changes

tradeoff_points.parameter: Integration tightness between new and legacy code
conflicting_attributes: Performance (positive: tighter integration reduces overhead)
Extensibility (negative: tighter integration makes future additions more difficult)

scenario_reference.text: System operates seamlessly across Linux and Windows NT platforms without platform-specific code duplication

analysis_records.environment: Multi-platform deployment with Linux and Windows NT systems
analysis_records.stimulus: System deployment or operation on either Linux or Windows NT platform
analysis_records.response_measure: Identical functionality and behavior on both platforms without duplicated code
architectural_decisions: Platform Abstraction Layer isolates platform-specific differences
Layered architecture ensures upper layers are platform-agnostic
Low-level I/O interface layer handles platform-specific operations
analysis_records.reasoning: The Platform Abstraction Layer is specifically designed to address this scenario by encapsulating platform differences. The architecture should allow all platform-specific code to be isolated in the abstraction layer, with upper layers remaining platform-independent. Success depends on the completeness of the abstraction and whether legacy components have platform-specific dependencies that cannot be easily abstracted.

risks.description: Legacy components may have platform-specific dependencies that are difficult to abstract
risks.rationale: Legacy code may directly use platform-specific APIs, file system structures, or threading models that cannot be fully abstracted without significant refactoring

risks.description: Platform Abstraction Layer may not cover all platform differences, requiring conditional code in upper layers
risks.rationale: Subtle platform differences in behavior, performance characteristics, or resource limits may leak through the abstraction, requiring platform-specific handling in upper layers

non_risks.description: Platform Abstraction Layer design specifically addresses cross-platform requirements
non_risks.rationale: The architecture includes a dedicated abstraction layer for isolating platform differences, providing a proven pattern for cross-platform support

non_risks.description: Layered architecture naturally supports platform independence in upper layers
non_risks.rationale: The hierarchical structure ensures upper layers interact only with abstracted interfaces, not platform-specific implementations

sensitivity_points.parameter: Completeness of Platform Abstraction Layer coverage
Number of platform-specific dependencies in legacy code

conflicting_attributes: Interoperability (positive: finer-grained abstraction covers more platform differences)
Performance (negative: finer-grained abstraction adds more overhead to low-level operations)

scenario_reference.text: System maintains stability and availability despite legacy code constraints and single-threaded control models

analysis_records.environment: Production environment with continuous operation requirements
analysis_records.stimulus: System operates under normal and stress conditions with legacy component constraints
analysis_records.response_measure: System maintains 99.5% availability and stable operation despite legacy limitations
architectural_decisions: Hybrid Legacy Integration Model isolates legacy component failures
N-Tier Architecture allows redundancy at each tier
Layered interfaces enable error handling and recovery at tier boundaries
analysis_records.reasoning: Reliability with legacy constraints requires careful error handling, isolation of legacy component failures, and architectural patterns that prevent single-threaded legacy components from becoming single points of failure. The layered architecture provides natural boundaries for implementing fault tolerance mechanisms. However, the single-threaded nature of legacy components limits concurrency-based reliability patterns.

risks.description: Single-threaded legacy components create single points of failure that cannot be easily mitigated through redundancy
risks.rationale: Single-threaded control models prevent active-active redundancy patterns; failures in legacy components may require full restart, causing service interruption

risks.description: Legacy code may lack robust error handling, causing failures to propagate through the system
risks.rationale: Legacy components may not implement modern error handling patterns, allowing exceptions or errors to cascade through integration boundaries

risks.description: Resource leaks in legacy components may accumulate over time, degrading stability
risks.rationale: Legacy code may have memory leaks or resource management issues that degrade system stability during long-running operations

non_risks.description: Layered architecture provides isolation boundaries for fault containment
non_risks.rationale: Tier boundaries allow failures to be caught and handled, preventing propagation to other layers

non_risks.description: Proven legacy code has demonstrated reliability in production
non_risks.rationale: The Hybrid Legacy Integration Model leverages legacy code that has been validated through operational use, reducing risk of unknown defects

sensitivity_points.parameter: Mean time between failures (MTBF) of legacy components
Error handling coverage at tier boundaries

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: automatic restart improves availability)
Performance (negative: restart causes service interruption and state loss)

scenario_reference.text: Overhead introduced by Platform Abstraction Layer for I/O-intensive operations remains below 10% across Linux and Windows NT platforms

analysis_records.environment: I/O-intensive workload on both Linux and Windows NT platforms
analysis_records.stimulus: High-volume I/O operations through Platform Abstraction Layer
analysis_records.response_measure: Platform Abstraction Layer overhead < 10% compared to native platform I/O performance
architectural_decisions: Platform Abstraction Layer uses thin wrapper approach for I/O operations
Low-level I/O interface minimizes abstraction overhead
Platform-specific optimizations within abstraction layer
analysis_records.reasoning: The 10% overhead target is achievable if the Platform Abstraction Layer uses thin wrappers that map directly to platform-native I/O calls. However, if the abstraction requires data transformation, buffering, or complex mapping logic, overhead may exceed 10%. The single-threaded nature of legacy components may also prevent the use of asynchronous I/O patterns that could reduce overhead.

risks.description: Complex abstraction logic or data transformations may cause overhead to exceed 10% target
risks.rationale: If the Platform Abstraction Layer must perform significant data conversion or buffering to reconcile platform differences, the overhead will accumulate with I/O volume

risks.description: Single-threaded legacy components prevent use of asynchronous I/O optimizations
risks.rationale: Asynchronous I/O patterns that could reduce overhead are incompatible with single-threaded control models, forcing synchronous I/O with higher overhead

non_risks.description: Thin wrapper approach can achieve low overhead for common I/O operations
non_risks.rationale: If the abstraction layer uses direct mapping to platform-native calls for common operations, overhead can be kept minimal

sensitivity_points.parameter: Complexity of platform difference reconciliation logic
I/O operation frequency and data volume

conflicting_attributes: Interoperability (positive: complete abstraction supports more platform differences)
Performance (negative: complete abstraction requires more overhead for edge cases)
